title: Power
subtitle: Power in Class 11 Physics — P = W/t = Fv

blocks.heading: The Idea
blocks.body:
Power is the rate of doing work — how much energy is transferred per second. Two machines can do the same total work, but the one that does it faster has more power.
In everyday life, power determines performance: a 100 W bulb consumes 100 J every second; a 150 W bulb consumes 150 J per second and is brighter. A 100 kW car engine can deliver 100 000 J of work per second to the wheels — enough to accelerate the car rapidly or maintain high speed against air resistance.
The commercial unit of energy — the kilowatt-hour (kWh) — is simply power × time: 1 kWh = 1000 W × 3600 s = 3.6 MJ.

blocks.heading: Average power:
blocks.body:
Instantaneous power (when force and velocity are both known):
where \theta is the angle between force and velocity.
Units:
SI: watt (W) = J/s = kg·m²·s⁻³
Practical: kilowatt (kW) = 1000 W
Old engineering: horsepower (hp): 1 hp ≈ 746 W
Energy from power over time:
Commercial electricity: 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J = 3.6 MJ

blocks.heading: Real-World Example
blocks.body: The human heart pumps about 5 litres of blood per minute against a pressure of ~100 mmHg (≈ 13 300 Pa). Power = pressure × flow rate ≈ 13 300 × (5 × 10⁻³ / 60) ≈ 1.1 W. Over a lifetime of 70 years, the heart does 1.1 \times 70 \times 365 \times 24 \times 3600 ≈ 2.4 GJ of work — without stopping once. An electric pump with the same power rating would need 24 kWh per year to match it, costing about ₹170 at typical electricity rates.

blocks.heading: Try It At Home
blocks.body: Time how long it takes you to climb a full flight of stairs (measure the height — count steps and multiply by ~17 cm per step). Calculate your power output: P = mgh/t. Compare to a typical household bulb (40–100 W). A healthy adult can sustain about 100 W for extended periods; trained athletes sustain 300–400 W and peak above 1000 W in short sprints.

blocks.heading: What If…
blocks.body: An electric motor has 80% efficiency. If it draws 500 W from the mains, how much useful mechanical power does it deliver? Where does the remaining 20% go?
